title: 2025/12/02 - FD (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-02 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of New Zealand Trade and Enterprise [Part 2]
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of New Zealand Trade and Enterprise
description: Tuesday 2 December 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee 2024/25 Annual review of New Zealand Trade and Enterprise Hearing of evidence: Submitter list: New Zealand Trade and Enterprise Pete Chrisp, Chief Executive NZTE Charles Finny, NZTE Board Rachel Baxter, General Manager - Strategy, People and Partners
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of New Zealand Trade and Enterprise. New Zealand Trade and Enterprise. Pete Chrisp, Chief Executive NZTE

chunks.text: All right, well, welcome along. Uh, we are live streaming now, I believe. Just one moment. Okay. That good? And Laura is online. Hey, Peter. Very good, thank you. Very good. Okay. Thank you. Hey, killer. Yeah, she was a moment ago on the, okay.

Well welcome along to the annual review of the 24 25 financial year for New Zealand Trade and Enterprise. We have 45 minutes set down for this evening's hearing if necessary. Uh, luck to hand over to you for introductory comments, and then I'm sure there will be questions from members. Thank you.

Uh, well thank you very much, chair for, for having us. And, uh, Damien, can I just say thank you very much for joining us on Thursday night? It occurred to me, um, as I was sitting in the airplane before, we actually should have invited all of you from the select committee to attend the International Business Awards because if you'd heard the stories they were telling about, um, companies were telling about, uh, how NZT has helped 'em, this would've been a very, very short session.

Uh, anyway, Damien was there to witness and thank you. It's appreciated. Um, so I, I'm, um, delighted to be speaking behalf of NZTE. I'm Charles Finney. I've been chair of NZTE since March. Um, and I've got the Chief executive Peter Crisp on my right, and I've got Rachel Baxter on my left.

Um, any difficult questions? I've got lots of support, but I, I was on the board for 12 years then, then, um, left for three years and back on. And so, uh, I'm very proud of NZT. It's an organization that I've, in terms of predecessor organizations, uh, worked alongside for many, many years now.

I've sort of been, um, part of the operation for many years and it keeps on getting better. And the story we're gonna tell today is, um, one, and we have a, um, can someone Collect this? Got a handout. We've got an Exhibit A, another one. Um, um, uh, which you can, you can study hard in a second.

Um, but, uh, in, in a rapidly changing global environment, international trade has never been more important to New Zealand's prosperity from food and fiber to tech, manufacturing, tourism and services exports are the cornerstone of our economic growth. With over $108 billion supporting one in four jobs, uh, growing exports is a key pillar of New Zealand's economic strategy under the government's aspiration to double the value of exports over 10 years.

NZT is supporting businesses to scale and to grow internationally for the good of New Zealand to help deliver a more prosperous, productive New Zealand economy. But it is really difficult out there. We have lots of challenges, mainly, um, uh, tariffs imposed by the United States and the distortions they're imposing.

And we have a slowing China, so it's not easy for our exporters. But we've had a really good year as NZTE achieving record results alongside a reduced budget. And this is why we're just giving you that chart, which exemplifies that fact. Uh, NZTE took up this challenge and focused on increasing productivity and having impact doing more for less.

NZT has helped businesses deliver deals that generated a record 4.83 billion in export value to New Zealand. A 27% increase over the previous financial year with a smaller operating budget. We achieved a record 3,832 International export growth outcomes. These are things such as new channels to market, employing people, finding partners.

We supported the delivery of 17 government led trade missions so far, this parliamentary term, helping to open doors for hundreds of business delegates and delivering over 200 commercial outcomes valued at more than $2 billion in projected trade value. And we achieved a positive 78 net promoter score from the export customers we work with, demonstrating they find significant value in our support.

On the investment side, we supported 85 investment deals that resulted in the potential direct economic impact of 5.4 billion against the target of 5 billion, an increase of 19% on the previous year. And the realization of a longstanding internal goal to achieve 5 billion, um, PDEI.

By 2025, we saw an 11% rise in the number of investors while the value of NZ T'S investment pipeline expanded by over 60 billion, a 334% increase on the previous year. And we supported the establishment of Invest New Zealand. In fact, Peter and I were just up in Auckland hearing the minister, um, give a speech and announce the new board of, uh, invest, uh, invest New Zealand.

Uh, so our work has almost been done in that regard. But we, we supported the establishment of Invest New Zealand as a new entity, as part of a joint project with mb with the launch occurring on one July, 2025. And while we received a reduction in funding of 3.5 million this financial year from 179.3 to $175.8 million, we also increased results in all areas by focusing on core business lifting productivity.

We're working hard to get more for less with investment in New Zealand now operating as a separate entity. And NZ t's return to a single mandate of supporting the growth of New Zealand exporters. We continue to deliver on our core strategy, focusing our support on the thousand most ambitious growth oriented companies where we have, we we can have the most impact.

We're focusing with more intensity on emerging high growth companies and rapidly growing larger high potential firms. Looking ahead, NZTE is laser focused on supporting the government's going for growth strategy, working closely with our partner agencies, MB MPI, tourism education and others, um, at co being another, uh, to grow exports while delivering real value to New Zealand's economy as part of a crosscutting government approach.

There's no doubt global headwinds are creating challenging economic conditions for our exporters, leading to market uncertainty, rising tariffs and supply chain issues. Our latest customer survey also showed that scaling and growing a business and accessing the funding and resources to grow are impeding our exporters.

There are bright spots too. The economy grew by 10, export economy grew by 10. I wish the economy grown by 10%. The export economy had grown by 10%. And FFY 25 business confidence and export expectations are trending upward. Free trade agreements, um, which are negotiated by our counterpart agency mfa, uh, are opening up new, new markets.

Our exporters are finding new high value niches to explore primary industry exports reached record highs in 2025, and tech and services continue to grow at pace. Technologically, we're entering a new frontier where investment in AI and digital infrastructure will determine our global competitiveness.

Helping companies navigate these challenges and leverage these opportunities remain central to our work for the good of New Zealand. Thank you. We look forward to trying to answer your questions. Excellent. Thank you very much for those comments. Uh, fisher honorable dam com. Uh, look, thank you very much and, and congratulations.

Great. Yeah, they should have been there too, but, um, uh, would've left them involved. Yeah. Can I just say, um, your point, I'll go straight to one of the issues you raised, um, to your, and that is that, uh, a lot of investment interest in electricity is that interest because we are coming into a, a market that is distorted in favor of all those, uh, generators that is, I heard the minister, um, at EU Business Summit say, come in, welcome investment into areas of monopolistic opportunity.

And so do you think that NDT should have a role in, in encouraging investment into monopolistic areas of our economy? Well, um, um, if this is a future focus question, which I think it is, um, I, I can, um, uh, kick for touch because we are no longer responsible for this.

This is, um, an invest NZ function now. Uh, and um, we actually had the minister and the, uh, new chair, um, presenting on, uh, on the strategy. And I think you would find that, um, there would be interested supporting, um, uh, energy investment if it is linked to things such as building new data centers.

So we're talking greenfield's new activity as opposed to, um, existing activity. Obviously, huge interest in geothermal in areas such as that. So, um, not really our core business anymore. I'm sorry. No, and I, I, I guess I come back to the enterprise one. It's, it's one that Pete and I have discussed on numerous occasions, and that is that the balance of trade and inter enterprise, so the one of developing enterprises and fostering them to grow and, and export, um, given that g is a core part of that, that's why I started in a rather blunt way to say,

what are the constraints preventing more development and growth, real growth of those enterprises in our economy? Well, Peter might want to, um, add to this, in fact, we were just talking about this very issue before, and it's certainly true that for a number of manufacturing companies in particular, uh, the increase in energy prices over the last year has been a huge constraint.

And if you look at all the statistics where we are seeing primary sector do very well, high tech do extremely well. Uh, the one area that is not growing as fast as we would like to see, in fact it's probably contracting, is manufacturing. But Pete, do you want to Reflect? Yeah. Uh, this morning the minister announced target areas for investing NZ uh, which if they focus on these areas, will be very good for building, uh, the economy, uh, for an export as well.

So infrastructure, renewable energy, uh, data processing, storage and ai and the development of the tech economy, uh, advanced manufacturing and processing. So the more FDI there is into that, uh, the more you're building up, uh, capacity in New Zealand and the minister recited, um, some stats just to show how low our FDI as a percentage of our economy is compared to the OECD, um, uh, average.

I think the other thing too is that, so that's on the investment side. Um, we actually really liked the, the five pillars that the current government are working to, um, science and innovation, uh, infrastructure, again, uh, uh, regulation, um, education and trade and investment.

I mean, they're all about building the capacity in the economy because the thing that I've learned in this role is that there's no one thing that's gonna build enterprises and help us grow. But any government trying to have an economic development strategy's gotta be touching on a whole number of areas. And that's, we like those five pillars. So it's just a reminder that this is an annual review, backwards looking.

Um, the first couple are free as we warm Into it, So we do need to have an I appreciate. So, Because it does come back to the energy one. And so then directly, how has the closure of Callahan affected the potential for us to grow?

Um, you might have to say something about this as well. So, um, look, a, a lot of the funding that Callahan was dispersing has gone to mb, uh, which is very helpful. So, and of course the RD tax credits are still there. Uh, and I think it's probably too early to say, um, what the impact of not having Callahan there.

Uh, if there is to be an impact, it'll be at the sort of seeding, uh, startup part of the ecosystem. But so far it's very, very early days, so it's very hard to say what, what the impact will be The supplementary. And there are other people sort of stepping into the vacuum as well. Supplementary, Dr. Lawrence, you know, Um, thank you Chair.

Just on the part you mentioned, both in terms of energy and electricity, but also in terms of manufacturing. I wanna check, so we've known since 2023 that EU is gonna introduce CAM. And this is a question for both nzt. 'cause I know that N Zts got some information on this as well as mfa, but what has NZT been doing this last year in preparation for people going into the full implementation of CCAM on the 1st of January, 2026?

And how have you been working with, for example, industries that are affected? What exporting into EU on having to deal with and manage that? Um, uh, we look very carefully at what, uh, the individual requirements are of the companies that are exporting, particularly the thousand that we're, that we double down on, that we commit 80% of our effort to 1000 companies.

A big chunk of those are going to Europe. And a big chunk of those are very, uh, sensitive to the su sustainability settings in Europe. Yeah. So we, uh, build up capability in New Zealand for companies. So we run, we've got people employed that advise companies on sustainability settings, uh, internationally, particularly in Europe.

And we've got practices and, and specialist advisors in Europe who are advising the New Zealand companies on, uh, sustainability credentials, uh, in, in Europe. Have you heard much like concerns from those exporters in general about the settings or because of the settings, everything you've been doing, people are pretty comfortable, even though there might be adjustments that needs to be made, sign the case to be board, et cetera.

Like what, what are people saying? Uh, I think, um, yeah, strong sustainability settings in Europe are both a threat and an opportunity. Yeah. Um, there's no doubt if you want to, yeah, particularly on food and beverage or manufacturing, if you want to climb into the European market, you have to chin the bar particularly into those big supply chains like the Tescos or the wait shows, things like that.

So there's no, no doubt about it. But also, um, 'cause it's such a, a high bar of chin coming from New Zealand, uh, you've got a good country story behind you and you've got some good sustainability settings behind you as well. So, so, uh, that's what, that's the threat and that's the opportunity. Uh, And I think New Zealand exporters obviously taking advantage of the opportunity.

We've got the free trade agreement recently negotiated, but over the last year, um, we've got 34.3% increase in exports to the eu. It's only gonna become a more important market for us. And exporters are very aware of the need to be seen to be sustainable. That's good to know. I think we said, I think, um, a o New Zealand published a report that 70% of the goods exporters are exporting to countries with the requirement for, uh, carbon credentials or climate credentials to be stated.

Uh, so that we know that's there. It's quite a big, uh, you know, requirement around the world. It has evened down a little bit, I think, yeah, that whole requirement for sustainability is proven that it's not a one single linear trend. It is cycling a bit, particularly as we had a high inflation period post COVID.

It definitely dampened down some of the sustainability, uh, signals, but I think over time those signals are still there. Dana Kirkpatrick. Thank You, Mr. Chair. Um, uh, thank you, um, for your time today. Um, look, uh, my question is just about the government's, uh, target to double the value of exports, um, in, in 10 years time.

Um, and I'm just interested in your view on, on how we're tracking, how we're going. I mean, I look at this, this is the positive. Um, but, uh, do you, do you think that, if that's possible, how, what are your views on where that's going? How we perform in a, in a previous chair role, we were given an even more ambitious target, and we delivered on it in five years earlier than the target.

So it can be done if you, um, develop the right strategies. Obviously NZTE is not solely responsible or being held fully accountable for delivering, um, on that goal. But it is, uh, sort of one of, uh, uh, beacons on the hill. We have a, at every board meeting a, uh, a three piece of paper with, um, uh, key indicators.

And we have the government's goal of doubling exports as one of those indicators we look at, at every board meeting. And I'm very pleased to report that we're on track. Good, but it's only two years in. But, but we're on track. I, I'm personally confident that we can achieve that. We're not gonna be able to achieve that just on volume.

We're gonna have to increase the value of some of our exports as well. Correct. And that, that, and luckily that's an area where actually a lot of the growth is occurring in high tech and services. Mm-hmm. Which is a great segue into my next, uh, question on that topic, which is, um, where do you see the opportunities for us as a country to double that value?

Because as you rightly say, we can't just keep progressing volume. Um, the value is the key word. Um, well, I, I see opportunity all over, which is why she'd been at the business awards. You'd come away inspired. One of the best things we do as a board and, and, and working with the team is going to visit these companies. And you come away hearing their stories and their plans.

And it's very, very common to, uh, to visit companies that actually have that doubling as part of their strategy or even more. We, we visited one company at the most recent board meeting in the Bail Plenty, which, which, um, currently exporting about 75, uh, million dollars a year. They're, they're on track to go to 150 million, and that's before the 2034 target.

So we, this, you know, I, I've got no doubt that we can do it at a, at a certain level, but we are going to have to, uh, continue to, uh, support particularly the high tech, um, and and services economy if we're gonna get to that, that target. And there's enormous opportunity in that space. Low hanging fruit obviously is in the tourism and education space where we haven't fully recovered from COVID, but the, the, the, um, the work that is going on, the, the incredible product that's being developed in, in those, um, uh, those two sectors, um, fills me

with Hope. Chief Executive. Yeah. Yeah. Um, I mean, the tech is probably the biggest change since I've been in my role. It's quite some time. Um, is the growth in the size of the tech, uh, sector. Um, you know, so Holger share zero, um, just say nothing efficient.

Public healthcare be around for a long time. That company reports a company growing Datacom, uh, mainframe, a services company, uh, you know, all pretty good growth stories outside of, of strict goods. Um, it's weightless, highly dependent though on the smarts and the economy, all sorts of other setting.

That's why I like those five pillars of the government strategy. 'cause this, you don't grow export economy just by focusing on markets. You gotta build the capacity back here in New Zealand. So tech sector has clearly got a lot of, a lot going for it. Um, and the food, it's really, it's the path to value the added value. We've got lots of get the business awards we saw, I don't know, picks peanut butter, um, you know, fix and file peanut butter, craft beer, got good healthy wine just letting go for a while.

So all that value add food and beverage, uh, to compliment some of our bigger, our bigger trades and, and meat, um, and dairy. Uh, yeah, Kiwi fruit's a great story. Uh, they're just going from strength to strength. So all across food and bread beverages quite a bit. The one I worry about the most is the manufacturing.

Um, but there's still lots of good manufacturing companies like the Hamilton Jets of the world. Um, but yeah, I, I'm old fashioned enough to think that I think, you know, any economic development strategy needs a focus on manufacturing at the heart of it. Um, and that's the, you know, if you look at the export stats, so those are the ones that struggled in COVID and they've struggled a little bit, uh, at the moment.

So it's the manufacturing one. We should be opportunity, but needs a lot of focus. Okay. And just one more last one, if it's okay. Um, and so in terms of, uh, to, to make the gains that we need to make and to support the industries and the sectors that you talk about, what is the, um, operating environment, uh, is there an operating environment change that we need to make as a country?

Uh, is it regulatory? Is it, um, innovation? Is it r and d? What, what is the, what is the change that you think will drive the greatest, um, I guess result? Well, it's multifaceted as, as Pete Peter says, you know, the government's got a buy five pillar strategy, but we're not going to be able to get there unless we have the infrastructure, uh, that we need to support, um, the expansion and trade.

And we're not gonna get there unless we have, uh, the education sector delivering those who are going to be working in a, in our companies. Uh, and it's not going to, uh, and they have to have technological skills, uh, as well. And r and D's gonna be critical. The manufacturing company that I've talked about before, which is about double.

They leave their, their value is as much a high tech, um, automation company now as it was just a manufacturing company. They've invested a huge amount, um, and the value of what they're producing as a result is going to be considerably more so.

So it's multifaceted. I'm confident we can get there, but we have to make sure that the overall infrastructure and environment is, is, uh, is in place to support that growth. Next Question. Honor what Dam O'Connor, Uh, look, thank you. And, and Pete, you spoke and referred to a number of food companies and other companies direct into market offering additional value.

Um, we've just seen the sell off ORs in the process of selling the consumer side of our single biggest company offshore, uh, $4 billion. So there's a couple of questions. Is that $4 billion part of the pipeline that you refer to here of the investment? And the second thing is, how do you think it's possible for those smaller companies to compete and succeed in the international marketplace where now single biggest company has basically given up in that area of adding value, um, into the consumer market?

I'll let you feel that one. Um, I don't particularly, uh, want to comment on the investment decisions and commercial decisions about any one particular company, uh, to any great extent. Uh, but I think there's one thing to be understood about that play as I understand it, is, um, the three parts.

They got commodities, business and ingredients business, and they have the consumer brands, the ingredients business is a value add business. You know, if you go up into China and you look at Fonterras application centers, they've got 500 people employed up there that are doing customized ingredients for uses by chefs, pastry kitchens, all this sort of stuff.

So it's not, that's a value add play. They just simply saying, look, they've got to, uh, double down on their investment and part of their value add operation, which was ingredients, uh, because they don't think they were there. I think they said they were, oh, they're not the best owner of consumer brands. Like they're all their commercial decisions. And I'm not gonna comment on anyone company's commercial decisions, but at least I could understand it when they explained it.

Can I, can I just Mm. Can I challenge that a little bit? Because, you know, you are tasked, you've got taxpayer money with an objective of doubling the value. Yeah. Uh, and you say you won't make comment on those decisions, but you are working directly with companies to encourage them to make the right decisions.

And yet when they make those, you don't make a comment now that this is taxpayer's investment and isn't there an obligation for us as a select committee to ensure that that's, you know, being used in the right way. 'cause actually by an international measure, our exports as a percentage of GDP is very low, or at the low end of the spectrum.

So shifting that will require some change. And I think it's, to be fair to the, the question before, what are the settings? 'cause we are just, we are told just bringing more investment into the country will help drive that. You say there are five pillars, I accept that. Um, but a lot of the stuff we get from government is that bring the foreign direct investment and we will grow our export economy.

I, I, I realize it's complex, but, um, you know, there should be some response. Well, I'll give you a, a counterfactual. Uh, so on, um, Monday, I think maybe last week, it's all blurring. Uh, I was, uh, at a function with the, um, governor of Shong Province, and we were celebrating a $260 million investment in a pet food factory, which is utilizing a closed dairy factory in South Auckland.

Or, or, or maybe North Wata. Not quite sure where the boundary lies. Um, and, um, you know, that, that, that is certainly a, um, a project NZT has been heavily involved in and supporting. Um, and it, and it, I think, um, is definitely gonna be adding value to our economy and shows how smart foreign investment can be.

In the case of, uh, the Fonterra sale, uh, I would comment that they haven't sold their complete brand business. I think that in certain markets such as China, they're still, still, um, in that, in that space. Um, and, um, uh, it's their decision. I'm not, were we involved in any way in No, I don't think we were involved in any way.

Fonterra is, uh, a company that we will work with in many places around the world, but it's not one of our focus 1000 companies. It's, it's too big. They can do everything we could do in terms of support. Mr. Chairman, can I just follow on, 'cause you make a valid point. I don't think anyone would deny the value of, of Greenfield's investment and a whole new proposition at nui, but it's the one of actually just taking over, not necessarily guaranteeing any added value to an existing asset.

And so do you think the decisions should be, uh, differentiated between greenfields and between, just to say, a transfer of ownership when it comes to investment? Okay. Um, through the chair. I mean, I think, uh, okay. Yeah, you go, yeah. Um, just talking backwards because we are now entering Yeah, I appreciate that. But looking backwards, um, uh, when NZT was running investment, uh, our calculation was based on a DEI calculation, which is direct economic impact.

So it was a calculation effectively of the number of jobs that were created, both direct, uh, indirect additional taxation, the additional ebitda. So you might have a very, very big sale of a company with no additional value with zero PDI zero DEI. Or you might have a company that, uh, a foreign that buys a company, but then invests a lot in it.

Uh, and you get a big uplift to the PDI. So the calculation was designed to cater for exactly what you're saying, which is to calculate the economic uplift, uh, for the country. But I think, you know, just to go back to the five pillars, I think, and go back to, um, Dana's sort of question, is that, um, and to be honest, every government that I've been working with on both sides of the house have had all had a multifaceted economic development strategy with science and innovation, and with education in it, with infrastructure, in it,

with trade, in it, with investment. There's been differences around the edges, but everyone I think has known for a long time that there's no silver bullet. Uh, you gotta pull on all these levers in a co certain way at the same time, that's the only way to get it. And that's all the experience from the, uh, small, vast economies internationally as they run coherent strategies where they're pulling on these leaders at the same time.

Next Question. Dr. Uh, thank you Chair. Just wanna first pick up on something you said in terms of you could have a high cell value, but the, what you are looking for is the DEI. Can I check? So in your report, for example, on page eight, you talk about, um, the fact that the export goods service earning, et cetera, year to March, 2025 is 46 billion.

And then later on you're talking about, and, and I could have that, that those two numbers may not cooperate at All. But then for example, on page 12, you are saying that the, the PDI investment value, the ones that ended either hand in is 5.4 billion when you have for, so $46 billion as export goods and BI is 5.4 billion.

Is that, it may not actually work that way, but is that the right proportion? Uh, just to explain the, um, there's two triangles on page seven, and thank you for reading the, uh, annual report because a lot of time, and if it goes into next things, I, I don't mind look at that. Highlight highlights A best Salad. You should, and go through and just highlight it so you can hold it up. Um, See that's where it says about the one.

Yeah. Very good. Um, anybody's read, page seven is the International Revenue of Companies, uh, just accumulated, uh, the graph on, which is the same one we've handed out. Yeah. Uh, the, the graph on the investment PDI is, um, the underlying investment value, uh, multiplied by the value of that investment.

So that's the, okay. The impact of that investment. So for example, you're running a value of two to one, uh, then 5.4 would be, um, you know, two and a half billion dollars worth of underlying deals to generate $5.4 billion worth of, uh, value. Yeah. Uh, so it's a, a different part of the equation.

So that's not, um, that those aren't, isn't the actual ratio, but that just gives you an i, uh, idea of how it works. And just one final question, chair, and, and this is sort of related. Um, one of the things obviously that has been giving, and you mentioned wine before, uh, giving a lot of, um, uncertainty to the export sector is the US tariffs.

Um, I wanna check what's entity's role in this? Um, and especially how you are working with the export sector, those who are affected in navigating the change in systems with the tariffs, particularly since some of how tariff manifests itself in the US and both from, uh, import into us, but also a supply chain issue.

Yeah. Okay. So, um, I think we might well have a go at answering that question at a, at a headline level. Obviously the, the, um, response, uh, in terms of policy is not our responsibility. That's an and ministerial Yeah. Responsibility. But we are doing everything we can to, to support New Zealand companies navigate these increasingly choppy waters.

Would you like to just talk about the tariff, The support, Rachel, go? I think There's a, a few things we're doing, one we've been running a lot of webinars with, and I think we've had the highest turnout to those webinars than worried webinars. Um, we've run, so that's just general kind of information. We've also brought in a one-to-one service.

We, we can be working directly with a company, um, exactly like you're saying, thinking about supply chain, thinking about scenario planning. Um, and then there's been a, you know, bunch of things we've been doing in terms of Moment committee, happy to give leave for the ceo, you'll end up At his blog. I warn you. We've done that. I think we've done that carry. Yep. And A bunch of kind of digital content and so on.

And, um, mine, ZT platform as well. So it's been a combination of one to some, one to many, and then one-to-one work with Customers. And what's the response, I guess, and satisfaction? Yeah, in terms of the quality of advice that's Been presented. Um, so satisfaction's been really good. Um, I think companies are hungry for context and information.

We are certainly not seeing companies make any big, um, changes at the moment in terms of moving supply chains or anything because it's just been such an uncertain environment. So companies have been, um, typically, you know, staying still, but really kind of planning what's plan A, what's plan B, uh, what's plan C.

So can I just give you a, a case study which we, we met with recently. And so it's a company, the same company actually that, that $75 million turnover, it's got a manufacturing operation in the states, one in the UK and one in New Zealand. They thought that they would be pretty much immune from the tariffs because of the fact they're manufacturing the United States, but they have discovered, um, they're getting a double whammy.

So every input that they are having to import is obviously more expensive with, with, um, the tariffs. Some of what they're using is, um, a steel product, so it's not just a 15% uplift, it's a 50% or 45% uplift. Um, and all the prices of US inputs have gone up as well.

So, uh, in terms of tariffs, they're finding that they've got a $2 million extra tariff bill in that since the tariffs were introduced. And they've also got the uncertainty of, um, every import uh, shipment is getting a different interpretation at the port. And so they've had to employ a staff member who does nothing but dealing with the customer's administration.

So you've got the $2 million plus you've got, got the cost of that staff member. So that, I think that's a reasonably typical, uh, story.
